content:
{
  "title": "Counterfactual Prompting for Robust Code Generation in Large Language Models",
  "problem": "Current code generation prompting strategies rely heavily on in-distribution, syntactically-correct code exemplars, limiting robustness to diverse or erroneous user inputs. This results in degraded generation quality when prompts contain uncommon or flawed code fragments.",
  "motivation": "Real-world users often provide prompts that deviate from training distributions, including incomplete, buggy, or unconventional code snippets. Improving LLM resilience to such scenarios is critical as code generation tools are deployed in varied and less-controlled environments. Addressing prompt robustness can expand LLM utility for a broader range of developers.",
  "method": "We propose a counterfactual prompting method, where during inference, the model is fed with prompts augmented by minimally perturbed (e.g., off-by-one errors, swapped operations, or variable renaming) versions of the original code snippets. By explicitly juxtaposing the original and counterfactual prompts, the model is encouraged to 'explain away' the perturbation and focus on the intended semantic task. This structured contrast aims to guide the LLM towards more accurate and resilient code generation, even from flawed or noisy prompts.",
  "experiment_plan": "We evaluate our method on HumanEval and MBPP for Python code generation, and use both clean and systematically perturbed user prompts. Baselines include standard few-shot prompting and chain-of-thought prompting. Metrics include functional correctness (pass@k), edit distance from ground-truth, and robustness under noisy prompt conditions."
}
